title: No Exceptions
orientation: Abolitionists Rising's pillar statement against any exceptions to abortion abolition (rape, incest, fetal disability, life of the mother), grounding the case in the image of God, the gospel, and historical abolitionist warnings against compromise.
audiences: rape or incest survivor weighing the argument

questions.question: Why do abolitionists insist that abortion be abolished totally, without any exceptions?
questions.answer: Because abortion is murder in every case, and the same moral logic that demands its immediate abolition also demands its total abolition. Carving out exceptions would be admitting that some children may be murdered based on circumstance, which contradicts the very reason abolition is required.
questions.quote: For the same reasons that we demand abortion’s immediate abolition, we demand abortion’s total abolition. That means no exceptions. No exceptions which allow for murdering babies because of the circumstances in which they were conceived. No exceptions that allow for murdering babies because they have a disease or disorder. No exceptions whatsoever. If abortion is murder, and we all know it is, it must be abolished entirely.

questions.question: Isn't accepting some exceptions a pragmatic way to make progress against abortion?
questions.answer: No. The article argues that exceptions are not only morally repugnant but also pragmatically devastating, because compromising on a capital evil destroys the moral authority that drives the cause forward.
questions.quote: Not only are exceptions morally repugnant, they are devastating to the cause pragmatically. To compromise on an issue like child sacrifice is to fatally weaken the foundation of the moral argument.

questions.question: What does Elizabeth Heyrick's warning to slavery abolitionists have to do with abortion exceptions?
questions.answer: Heyrick observed that compromise weakened, rather than strengthened, the anti-slavery cause. The article applies the same lesson: every concession on abortion erodes the conviction that abolitionists are right and ultimately undermines their success.
questions.quote: On this great questions, the spirit of accommodation and conciliation has been a spirit of delusion. The abolitionists have lost, rather than gained ground by it; their cause has been weakened, instead of strengthened. The great interests of truth and justice are betrayed, rather than supported, by all softening qualifying concessions. Every iota which is yielded of their rightful claims, impairs the conviction of their rectitude, and, consequently, weakens their success.

questions.question: How is the abolitionist case against abortion different from the standard pro-life case?
questions.answer: The pro-life movement appeals to suffering, heartbeat, or harm to women, while abolitionism grounds opposition in the image of God in human beings and in Christ's incarnation to rescue mankind, making it a gospel conflict with the evils of the age rather than a political argument.
questions.quote: While the pro-life movement will argue that abortion is wrong because it causes pain, stops a beating heart, or betrays women, abolitionists maintain that abortion is wrong because human beings are created in the image of God and because the Creator Himself became a man in order to rescue mankind from sin, death, and eternal separation from God.

questions.question: Does a child conceived in rape or incest forfeit the right to live?
questions.audience: rape or incest survivor weighing the argument
questions.answer: No. The article frames any exception based on the circumstances of conception as murder, since the child's humanity is not contingent on how he or she came to exist. The article groups rape and incest exceptions with all other circumstantial exceptions that must be rejected.
questions.quote: No exceptions which allow for murdering babies because of the circumstances in which they were conceived.

questions.question: What about a child diagnosed with a serious disability or genetic abnormality?
questions.answer: A shorter or harder life does not justify killing the child. The article calls this exception facially evil and heartless toward both the preborn child and disabled people who are already born.
questions.quote: Regarding physical or genetic abnormalities, the fact that a child might have a shorter or more difficult life does not justify the right to murder them. This exception for abortion is so facially evil, absurd, and heartless toward the preborn child as well as born people with disabilities that it’s hard to believe anyone makes it.

questions.question: What if continuing the pregnancy puts the mother's life in danger?
questions.answer: The article concedes that early delivery may sometimes be necessary, lowering the child's odds of survival, but insists intentional killing is never permitted. Doctors must remain healers, not executioners.
questions.quote: Regarding situations where the life of the mother is in jeopardy, there is no circumstance where the baby must be intentionally murdered. There are cases where the child must be delivered early, and in those cases, the child may have a lower probability of survival than a child born at full-term, but intentional murder must not be allowed as an option. Doctors must be healers, not killers.

questions.question: Why does the article call exception-based compromise 'double-minded wickedness'?
questions.answer: Because if abortion truly is murder, then negotiating exceptions treats a capital crime like ordinary politics. That posture betrays the very truth that justifies opposing abortion in the first place.
questions.quote: To compromise with and allow exceptions for a capital crime would be double-minded wickedness. To make concessions with an evil such as slavery or abortion as if issues of murder and dehumanization were like any other political back-and-forth is to weaken the power that truth and justice hold over the discussion.

questions.question: What does it actually mean to be an 'abolitionist' rather than just someone who wants abortion to end?
questions.answer: Abolitionism is more than a goal of ending a practice. It is a confrontation between God's revealed truth and the evils and worldly wisdom of the age, modeled on figures like William Lloyd Garrison who sought to make Christianity the enemy of all sin.
questions.quote: To be an abolitionist is more than simply working to end something. Anyone can work to end something. Abolitionism is about bringing the truth revealed in God’s word into conflict with the evils of the age and the worldly wisdom of man.

questions.question: How does William Lloyd Garrison's posture inform the abolitionist stance against exceptions?
questions.answer: Garrison framed his work as making Christianity the enemy of everything sinful, which the article cites as the model for refusing any compromise with abortion. Successful human rights campaigns have historically rested on this uncompromising foundation.
questions.quote: Successful human rights campaigns throughout history have been built on this foundation. Perhaps the best articulation of what it means to bring the Gospel into conflict with the evil of the age comes from slavery abolitionist William Lloyd Garrison when he said, “My fanaticism is to make Christianity the enemy of all that is sinful.”

questions.question: What is the article's closing call to action for those who oppose abortion?
questions.answer: Reject every compromise on dehumanization, demand equal justice and protection for all human beings, and pursue total and immediate abolition. Any spirit of accommodation strips the movement of the power of truth and justice.
questions.quote: Never compromise on dehumanization. Demand equal justice and protection for all human beings, meaning abortion's total and immediate abolition. To have a spirit of compromise, or accommodation and conciliation as Heyrick put it, is to fight against abortion without the power of truth and justice. God forbid we ever do so.

related.title: How Shall an Abolitionist Vote?
related.reason: Directly engages Trump's rape/incest/life-of-mother exceptions and how an abolitionist should respond to candidates who endorse them.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Lays out the structural case against legislative incrementalism, the policy mechanism by which exception-laden bills get passed.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Companion argument to total abolition, explaining the 'immediate' half of the immediate-and-total claim invoked in this article.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Expands the contrast the article draws between abolitionist gospel-grounded reasoning and pro-life movement reasoning.

related.title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
related.reason: Detailed rebuttal of the Christian intellectual case for accepting exceptions and compromise legislation.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Argues the pragmatic harm of compromise, complementing this article's claim that exceptions are 'devastating to the cause pragmatically.'

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Theological grounding for why God's holiness forbids the qualifying concessions Heyrick warned about.
